headline: Tim Babcock Becomes Governor After Nutter's Death, 1962
body:
The wind howled, a savage beast tearing at the Montana sky. January 25, 1962. A date etched in the cold, unforgiving rock of history. Governor Donald Grant Nutter, a man forged in the fires of war, a B-24 pilot who had cheated death across 62 combat missions, now faced a foe more relentless than any enemy fighter: the raw, untamed fury of a Montana blizzard. He was en route to Cut Bank, a speaking engagement awaiting, a future he would never see. The C-47 Skytrain, a workhorse of the skies, groaned against the gale. Its metal skin shivered, a fragile shell against the monstrous force. Winds, a hundred miles per hour and more, clawed at the aircraft, seeking purchase, seeking weakness. And they found it. A wing, torn away, a brutal amputation in the black, frozen night. The plane, suddenly unbalanced, screamed its death song, a metallic shriek as it plunged earthward. Wolf Creek Canyon, a jagged maw in the landscape, rose to meet it. Thirty-five miles north of Helena, the impact was a thunderclap, a violent punctuation mark in the silence of the wilderness. Burning gasoline painted the snow-laden trees in grotesque, flickering light. Wreckage, twisted and broken, scattered for four hundred yards down the steep, unforgiving slope. Six bodies, Nutter, his executive secretary Dennis Gordon, State Agriculture Commissioner Edward Wren, and three brave Air National Guardsmen—Major Clifford Hanson, Major Joseph Devine, and Master Sgt. Charles Ballard—were flung into the frozen embrace of the earth. The other wing, a ghost in the storm, drifted to the ground a mile away, almost untouched, a silent witness to the carnage. 

Lieutenant Governor Tim Babcock, a man who knew the skies, had felt the chill of foreboding that very day. A Great Falls Tribune article, dated January 27, 1962, would later quote him, recalling his words to a Billings friend just an hour before the flight: “I don’t like the idea of Don flying in those obsolescent airplanes.” He had spoken of the smaller C45 Beechcraft, but the sentiment, a premonition of disaster, hung heavy in the air. Babcock, a private pilot himself, had shaken his head slowly, a silent acknowledgment of the dangers that lurked in the winter skies. Nutter, in the end, had taken the larger C47, the civilian DC3’s military twin, believing it safer. He was wrong. 

Nutter, a man of the people, born in Lambert, a veteran of the Montana Senate, had ascended to the governorship in 1960. His tenure, though brief, was marked by a fierce dedication to fiscal responsibility and the promotion of industry. He had reduced state spending, championed economic development, and sought to modernize the state government. His vision, however, was cut short, snuffed out by the indifferent cruelty of the elements. His casket lay in state in the Capitol House Chambers, a somber tableau alongside those of Wren and Gordon, guarded by the silent sentinels of the Montana National Guard. Three thousand souls braved the cold, gray skies and raw wind of Sidney for his funeral, a testament to the man and the shock that gripped the state. Former President Eisenhower sent a four-foot white cross, a stark symbol of remembrance. Two thousand hushed persons moved past the caskets in the first three afternoon hours, their grief a palpable weight in the air. The death of Governor Nutter was a shock to Montana, a sudden, brutal reminder of the thin line between life and oblivion in the wild, beautiful, and often merciless landscape of the state. It was a decade of shocks, and this was but the first, a chilling prelude to the national tragedy that would follow less than two years later.
